title: Vegyn: The Producer Who Stayed
author: Allastair Voss
body:
There are producers who chase the spotlight and producers who feed the people who stand in it. Joe Thornalley, working as Vegyn, has spent a decade being extraordinarily good at the second one while also making some of the most interesting solo work no one talks about.

You know his work even if you don't know his name. He contributed production to Frank Ocean's Blonde and Endless, the two records that reshaped what an R&B album could look like in 2016. He hosted Blonded Radio. He played the PrEP+ nights in New York. He moved quietly through a moment that was very loud, left fingerprints on it, and then went back to London.

[spotify:artist/5iUnvXddCpOrbWKm7QMr6o]

His own label, PLZ Make It Ruins, has been operating since 2014. It started as a record label and became something more porous, a space for releasing music but also for thinking about what releasing music means when the industry has collapsed into streams and algorithmic placement. Vegyn has been deliberately contrary about it in a way that does not feel performative. He is not making a statement. He is just not playing the game.

## The Blonde Connection and What It Cost Him

The Frank Ocean collaboration is the most discussed fact about Vegyn's career, and it is worth examining what kind of fact it is. Contributing to Blonde and Endless brought significant attention, but it did not translate into the commercial trajectory that collaborating with that scale of artist usually produces. Thornalley did not follow the work with a push toward mainstream production, high-profile co-writes, or industry relationship cultivation. He went back to PLZ Make It Ruins and kept making the records he wanted to make.

This is not a story of failure to capitalize. It is a story of not wanting to capitalize in that direction. The distinction matters because the music that resulted, the solo work under the Vegyn name, could not have been made by someone who was optimizing for broad appeal. It required the freedom that comes from having no particular audience to please.

The producers who take the commercial path after a major collaboration credit are making a reasonable choice. The industry rewards it. Thornalley made a different choice, and the result is a body of work that belongs to a different tradition entirely, one that includes artists who prioritize the internal logic of a record over its external legibility.

## The Solo Discography

His solo discography reflects this. Don't Follow Me Because I'm Lost Too!!, a sprawling 2022 release, dropped with almost no promotional apparatus and turned out to be one of the more fascinating electronic records of that year, dense, melodic, occasionally overwhelming, and deeply unhurried. Tracks like Skullflower and Debold operate with a logic that is hard to trace but easy to feel.

The record is long. It makes demands. It refuses to be useful background music. Those qualities are not accidents. They are choices about what the album is for: not for casual consumption, not for streaming platforms, not for listeners who want to feel comfortable. For listeners who want to feel something they have not felt before.

Thornalley's production approach on the solo work is maximalist in its information density but restrained in its emotional temperature. There is a lot happening in these tracks at any given moment, but none of it is designed to produce the obvious responses. The satisfaction is oblique and more durable than a hit record can provide.

The sequencing on Don't Follow Me is another deliberate choice. The album does not front-load its strongest moments. It distributes them with the logic of a long walk rather than a highlight reel, which means listeners who make it through have received something qualitatively different from listeners who sampled three minutes and moved on. Most records are designed to hook you in the first ninety seconds. This one is designed for the person who stays.

## The Headache Project

His Headache project with vocalist MIC delivered Thank You for Almost Everything in late 2025, another record that refuses to announce itself. It is warmer than his solo work, structured around something close to pop songwriting, and easier to hold, though not easier to understand.

MIC's vocals function as a counterweight to Thornalley's production instincts. Where the Vegyn solo work can feel cool to the point of distance, the Headache records locate warmth without softening the formal intelligence. The collaboration has produced music that can sit alongside the work of artists like Arthur Russell, for whom intimacy and formal strangeness were always the same thing.

The vocal production on Thank You for Almost Everything is particularly strong. The voice is treated as a texture as much as a carrier of meaning, sometimes buried in reverb and layered, sometimes completely present and unprocessed. The variance creates a record that keeps revealing itself on repeated listens, which is the only kind of album that justifies the format.

## The PLZ Make It Ruins Infrastructure

The label itself is a statement about how music can be released. PLZ Make It Ruins has issued records with minimal press, minimal promotion, and no particular concession to how the streaming era expects artists to behave. This is not impractical idealism. It is a coherent position about what the work is for and who it belongs to.

Most independent labels at this scale eventually compromise toward visibility or viability. PLZ Make It Ruins has not. That is either stubborn or principled, and from the outside it looks like both. The catalog it has built is a genuine archive of a specific kind of intelligence about what electronic music can be.

## Why the Obscurity Is Coherent

Vegyn does not seem interested in being discovered. That's fine. The work is there when you go looking for it, and it holds up every time.

The broader lesson from his career is that the relationship between visibility and quality is not fixed. Some of the most interesting work in any given decade belongs to artists operating at a deliberate remove from the attention economy. That remove is not a failure to connect. It is a different kind of connection, slower and more durable, between the work and the people who need it specifically.
